title: K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) & Naive Bayes Algorithms
learning_outcomes: Formulate and calculate Minkowski distance metrics and analyze the geometry of the Curse of Dimensionality.
Contrast the partition structures and search complexities of KD-Trees and Ball-Trees for spatial indexing.
Formulate the Naive Bayes conditional independence assumption and derive the Laplace smoothed parameter estimators.

content_sections.title: K-Nearest Neighbors and Distance Geometry
content_sections.content:
The K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) algorithm is a non-parametric, instance-based learning model. Unlike parametric models, KNN does not learn a set of weights during training; instead, it stores the training dataset directly in memory, deferring all computational work to the inference phase (lazy learning). Given a query point $\mathbf{x}_q \in \mathbb{R}^d$, the algorithm identifies the $k$ closest observations in the training set and assigns the target class via majority voting.

The definition of 'closeness' is determined by the choice of distance metric. The general metric for continuous spaces is the **Minkowski distance** of order $p$, defined as:

$$D_p(\mathbf{x}_a, \mathbf{x}_b) = \left( \sum_{i=1}^d |x_{a,i} - x_{b,i}|^p \right)^{1/p}$$

This generalizes standard distance formulations:
1.  **Manhattan Distance ($p=1$):** L1 norm, measuring distances along orthogonal axes.
2.  **Euclidean Distance ($p=2$):** L2 norm, measuring straight-line distance.
3.  **Chebyshev Distance ($p \to \infty$):** Maximum absolute coordinate difference: $\max_i |x_{a,i} - x_{b,i}|$.

In high-dimensional spaces, KNN suffers from the **Curse of Dimensionality**. As the dimension $d$ grows, the volume of the space increases exponentially, causing the data to become extremely sparse. Geometrically, the ratio of the distance to the nearest neighbor and the distance to the furthest neighbor approaches 1:

$$\lim_{d \to \infty} \frac{D_{\max} - D_{\min}}{D_{\min}} = 0$$

Consequently, every sample becomes nearly equidistant to every other sample, neutralizing the concept of 'nearest' neighbors.

callouts.title: Scaling Before Distance Evaluation
callouts.content: KNN is highly sensitive to the scale of features. If one feature has a range of $[0, 100000]$ (e.g. annual salary) and another has a range of $[0, 1]$ (e.g. grade point average), the annual salary will dominate the distance computation, rendering the GPA feature useless. Always apply normalization (Min-Max) or standardization (Z-score) before running distance-based algorithms.

content_sections.title: Accelerated Nearest Neighbors: KD-Trees and Ball-Trees
content_sections.content:
A naive implementation of KNN computes the distance between the query point $\mathbf{x}_q$ and all $N$ training samples, resulting in a computational complexity of $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot d)$ per prediction, which is unacceptable for large-scale production serving. To accelerate search queries, we build spatial indexing trees:

### KD-Trees (k-dimensional trees)
A **KD-Tree** is a binary search tree that recursively partitions the $d$-dimensional space along axis-aligned hyperplanes. At each level of the tree, the algorithm selects the median value along a single feature dimension to split the data. The average query time complexity is reduced to $\mathcal{O}(\log N \cdot d)$. However, when the dimension $d$ is high (typically $d > 20$), the query search must back-track through almost all branches, causing the complexity to degrade back to the naive $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot d)$ scan.

### Ball-Trees
To scale in higher dimensions, **Ball-Trees** partition the space using a series of nesting hyperspheres (balls) rather than axis-aligned hyperplanes. Each node in the tree is defined by a centroid and a radius. During query search, if the distance from the query point to the centroid of a candidate ball is greater than the radius plus the active minimum distance, the entire branch is pruned. This pruning method remains robust in high-dimensional manifolds.

callouts.title: Inference Latency in Production Pipelines
callouts.content: In high-throughput pipelines where latency must be under 10 ms (e.g., real-time search scoring), exact nearest neighbor searches should be replaced with Approximate Nearest Neighbors (ANN) libraries like Faiss or HNSW. ANN sacrifices a minor degree of accuracy to achieve sub-millisecond search latency.

content_sections.title: Naive Bayes and Laplace Smoothing
content_sections.content:
The Naive Bayes classifier is a probabilistic model based on Bayes' Theorem. Given a class label $Y \in \{c_1, \dots, c_K\}$ and a feature vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, \dots, x_d]^{\top}$, the posterior probability is:

$$P(Y = c_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid Y = c_k) P(Y = c_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

To compute the joint likelihood $P(\mathbf{x} \mid Y = c_k) = P(x_1, \dots, x_d \mid Y = c_k)$, we introduce the **conditional independence assumption**: we assume that every feature $x_i$ is conditionally independent of all other features given the class label $Y$. This simplifies the joint probability to a product of marginals:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid Y = c_k) = \prod_{i=1}^d P(x_i \mid Y = c_k)$$

This is the 'naive' assumption. The decision boundary is calculated by maximizing the numerator:

$$\hat{y} = \operatorname{argmax}_{c_k} \left[ P(Y = c_k) \prod_{i=1}^d P(x_i \mid Y = c_k) \right]$$

### Laplace Smoothing
For categorical features, the maximum likelihood estimate of probability $P(x_i = v_j \mid Y = c_k)$ is the frequency ratio $\frac{N_{k,j}}{N_k}$, where $N_{k,j}$ is the count of feature $x_i = v_j$ in class $c_k$, and $N_k$ is the total count of class $c_k$. If a feature value never occurs in the training split for a specific class (e.g. $N_{k,j} = 0$), the product probability falls to exactly zero, blocking the entire posterior prediction. To prevent this, we apply **Laplace smoothing**:

$$\hat{P}(x_i = v_j \mid Y = c_k) = \frac{N_{k,j} + \alpha}{N_k + \alpha \cdot V_i}$$

where $\alpha > 0$ is the smoothing parameter (typically $\alpha = 1$), and $V_i$ is the number of unique categories for feature $x_i$. This shifts probability mass from frequent observations to unobserved cells, guaranteeing non-zero probabilities.

callouts.title: Naive Bayes Independence in Practice
callouts.content: A common misconception is that Naive Bayes performs poorly when features are correlated. While correlated features distort the exact posterior probability value $P(Y \mid \mathbf{x})$, the classification decision boundary (ranking of classes) remains highly optimal in practice. This is because the correlation affects all classes symmetrically, keeping the class argmax boundaries stable.

content_sections.title: Professional Practice and Interview Insights
content_sections.content:
In engineering interviews, candidates are often asked about the different variants of Naive Bayes and when to use them:
1.  **Gaussian Naive Bayes:** Used when features are continuous. We assume features follow a normal distribution within each class: $x_i \mid Y=c_k \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_{k,i}, \sigma^2_{k,i})$.
2.  **Multinomial Naive Bayes:** Used for discrete count data (e.g., term frequency vectors in text classification). The feature probabilities represent multinomial parameters.
3.  **Bernoulli Naive Bayes:** Used for binary features (e.g. word presence/absence flags). The features are modeled as independent Bernoulli trials.

callouts.title: Underflow Prevention in Probability Products
callouts.content: During interviews, emphasize that multiplying numerous probabilities (values $\le 1$) leads to numerical underflow. In production systems, we calculate in the log-probability domain: $\log P(Y) + \sum \log P(x_i \mid Y)$, converting products into sums to ensure floating-point stability.
